**Military history** is the study of [armed conflict](/wiki/War "War") in the [history of humanity](/wiki/Human_history "Human history"), and its impact on the societies, cultures and economies thereof, as well as the resulting changes to [local](/wiki/Politics "Politics") and [international relationships](/wiki/International_relations "International relations").

The essential subjects of military history study are the causes of war, the social and cultural foundations, [military doctrine on each side](/wiki/Doctrine#Military_usage "Doctrine"), the logistics, leadership, technology, [strategy](/wiki/Strategy "Strategy"), and [tactics](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics") used, and how these changed over time. On the other hand, [just war theory](/wiki/Just_war_theory "Just war theory") explores the moral dimensions of warfare, and to better limit the destructive reality caused by war, seeks to establish a doctrine of military ethics.

As an applied field, military history has been studied at academies and service schools because the [military command](/wiki/Command_and_control_(military) "Command and control (military)") seeks to not repeat past mistakes, and improve upon its current performance by instilling an ability in commanders to perceive historical parallels during a battle, so as to capitalize on the lessons learned from the past. When certifying military history instructors[[1]](#cite_note-1) the [Combat Studies Institute](/wiki/Combat_Studies_Institute "Combat Studies Institute") deemphasizes rote detail memorization and focuses on themes and context in relation to current and future conflict, using the motto "Past is Prologue."[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The discipline of military history is dynamic, changing with development as much of the subject area as the societies and organisations that make use of it.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The dynamic nature of the discipline of military history is largely due to the rapid change of military forces, and the art and science of managing them, as well as the frenetic pace of [technological development](/wiki/Technological_evolution "Technological evolution") that had taken place during the period known as the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), and more recently in the [nuclear](/wiki/Nuclear_proliferation "Nuclear proliferation") and [information ages](/wiki/Information_age "Information age"). An important recent concept is the [Revolution in Military Affairs](/wiki/Revolution_in_Military_Affairs "Revolution in Military Affairs") (RMA) which attempts to explain how warfare has been shaped by emerging technologies, such as gunpowder. It highlights the short outbursts of rapid change followed by periods of relative stability.

## Popular versus academic military history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Popular versus academic military history")]

In terms of the history profession in major countries, military history is an orphan, despite its enormous popularity with the general public. [William H. McNeill](/wiki/William_H._McNeill_(historian) "William H. McNeill (historian)") points out:

:   This branch of our discipline flourishes in an intellectual ghetto. The 144 books in question [published in 1968-78] fall into two distinct classes: works aimed at a popular readership, written by journalists and men of letters outside academic circles, and professional work nearly always produced within the military establishment.... The study of military history in universities remains seriously underdeveloped. Indeed, lack of interest in and disdain for military history probably constitute one of the strangest prejudices of the profession.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In recent decades University level courses in military history remain popular; often they use films to humanize the combat experience. For example, Eugene P. A. Scleh, history professor at the University of Maine, has explored the advantages and problems of teaching a course of "Modern War and Its Images" entirely through films. Students said they found the documentaries more valuable than the dramas.[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, military historians are frustrated by their marginal status in major history departments.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Academic historians concerned with military topics have their own scholarly organization, [Society for Military History](/wiki/Society_for_Military_History "Society for Military History"). Since 1937 it has published *The Journal of Military History*. Its four issues a year include scholarly articles reviews of new books, and a bibliography of new publications and dissertations. The Society has 2300 members, holds an annual convention, and gives out prizes for the best scholarship.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Historiography of military history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Historiography of military history")]

[Historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") is the study of the history and method of the discipline of history or the study of a specialised topic. In this case, military history with an eye to gaining an accurate assessment of conflicts using all available sources. For this reason military history is periodised, creating overlaying boundaries of study and analysis in which descriptions of battles by leaders may be unreliable due to the inclination to minimize mention of failure and exaggerate success. Military historians use Historiographical analysis in an effort to allow an unbiased, contemporary view of records.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

One military historian, Jeremy Black, outlined problems 21st-century military historians face as an inheritance of their predecessors: Eurocentricity, a technological bias, a focus on leading military powers and dominant military systems, the separation of land from sea and recently air conflicts, the focus on state-to-state conflict, a lack of focus on political "tasking" in how forces are used.[[11]](#cite_note-p.ix,_Black-11)

If these challenges were not sufficient for military historians, the limits of method are complicated by the lack of records, either destroyed or never recorded due to their value as a [military secret](/wiki/Military_secret "Military secret"). Scholars still do not know the exact nature of [Greek fire](/wiki/Greek_fire "Greek fire"), for instance. Researching Operation Enduring Freedom and Operation Iraqi Freedom, for example, have presented unique challenges to historians due to records that were destroyed to protect classified military information, among other reasons. Historians use their knowledge of government regulation and military organization, and employing a targeted and systematic research strategy to piece together war histories.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Despite these limits, wars are some of the most studied and detailed periods of human history.

Military historians have often [compared](/wiki/Comparative_history "Comparative history") organization, tactical and strategic ideas, leadership, and national support of the militaries of different nations.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the early 1980s, historian [Jeffrey Kimball](/wiki/Jeffrey_Kimball_(historian) "Jeffrey Kimball (historian)") studied the influence of a historian's political position on current events on interpretive disagreement regarding the causes of 20th century wars. He surveyed the ideological preferences of 109 active diplomatic historians in the United States as well as 54 active military historians. He finds that their current political views are moderately correlated with their historiographical interpretations. A clear position on the left-right continuum regarding capitalism was apparent in most cases. All groups agreed with the proposition, "historically, Americans have tended to view questions of their national security in terms of such extremes as good vs. evil." Though the Socialists were split, the other groups agreed that "miscalculation and/or misunderstanding of the situation" had caused U.S. interventionism." Kimball reports that:

:   Of historians in the field of diplomatic history, 7% are Socialist, 19% are Other, 53% are Liberal, 11% are None and 10% Conservative. Of military historians, 0% are Socialist, 8% are Other, 35% are Liberal, 18% are None and 40% are Conservative.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Online resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Online resources")]

People interested in military history from all periods of time, and all subtopics, are increasingly turning to the Internet for many more resources than are typically available in nearby libraries. Since 1993, one of the most popular sites, with over 4000 members (subscriptions are free) has been H-WAR, sponsored by the [H-Net](/wiki/H-Net "H-Net") network based at Michigan State University.[[15]](#cite_note-15) H-War has six coeditors, and an academic advisory board that sets policy. It sponsors daily moderated discussions of current topics, announcements of new publications and conferences, and reports on developments at conferences. The H-Net family of lists has sponsored and published over 46,000 scholarly book reviews, thousands of which deal with books in military history broadly conceived.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Wikipedia itself has a very wide coverage of military history, [with over 180,000 articles](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Military_history/Assessment#statistics "Wikipedia:WikiProject Military history/Assessment"). Its editors sponsor [Wikipedia:WikiProject Military history](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Military_history "Wikipedia:WikiProject Military history") and encourage readers to join.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Military and war museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Military and war museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Canadianwarmuseummgs.jpg/250px-Canadianwarmuseummgs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canadianwarmuseummgs.jpg)

The [Canadian War Museum](/wiki/Canadian_War_Museum "Canadian War Museum").

See also: [Category:Military and war museums](/wiki/Category:Military_and_war_museums "Category:Military and war museums")

Military museums specialize in military histories; they are often organized from a national point of view, where a museum in a particular country will have displays organized around conflicts in which that country has taken part. They typically take a broad view of warfare's role in the nation's history.[[18]](#cite_note-18) They typically include displays of weapons and other military equipment, [uniforms](/wiki/Military_uniform "Military uniform"), wartime [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda"), and exhibits on civilian life during wartime, and [decorations](/wiki/Military_orders,_awards_and_decorations "Military orders, awards and decorations"), among others. A military museum may be dedicated to a particular or area, such as the [Imperial War Museum Duxford](/wiki/Imperial_War_Museum_Duxford "Imperial War Museum Duxford") for military aircraft, [Deutsches Panzermuseum](/wiki/German_Tank_Museum "German Tank Museum") for tanks, the [Lange Max Museum](/wiki/Lange_Max_Museum "Lange Max Museum") for the [Western Front (World War I)](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)"), the [International Spy Museum](/wiki/International_Spy_Museum "International Spy Museum") for espionage, [The National World War I Museum](/wiki/Liberty_Memorial#The_National_World_War_I_Museum "Liberty Memorial") for [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the "D-Day Paratroopers Historical Center" (Normandy) for WWII airborne, or more generalist, such as the [Canadian War Museum](/wiki/Canadian_War_Museum "Canadian War Museum") or the [Musée de l'Armée](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_l%27Arm%C3%A9e "Musée de l'Armée"). For the Italian alpine wall one can find the most popular museum of bunkers in the small museum [n8bunker](https://www.n8bunker.com/) at Olang / Kronplatz in the heard of the dolomites of [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"). The U.S. Army and the state National Guards operate 98 military history museums across the United States and three abroad.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Curators debate how or whether the goal of providing diverse representations of war, in terms of positive and negative aspects of warfare. War is seldom presented as a good thing, but soldiers are heavily praised. David Lowenthal has observed that in today's museums, "nothing seems too horrendous to commemorate". Yet as Andrew Whitmarsh notes, "museums frequently portray a sanitised version of warfare."[[20]](#cite_note-20) The actual bomber that dropped the atomic bomb on Japan became the focus of an angry national controversy with veterans attacking curators and historians when the Smithsonian Institution planned to put its fuselage on public display in 1995. The [uproar](/wiki/Enola_Gay#Exhibition_controversy "Enola Gay") led to cancellation of the exhibit.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Early historians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early historians")]

The documentation of military history begins with the confrontation between [Upper and Lower Egypt](/wiki/Upper_and_Lower_Egypt "Upper and Lower Egypt") c. 3150 BC and [Sumer](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") (current [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq")) and [Elam](/wiki/Elam "Elam") (current [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")) c. 2700 BC near the modern [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra"). The Egyptian military scribe Tjaneni recorded the [Battle of Megiddo](/wiki/Battle_of_Megiddo_(15th_century_BC) "Battle of Megiddo (15th century BC)") (15th century BC) which is accepted as the first battle in relatively reliable detail.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Military details are abundant in [heroic epics](/wiki/Heroic_epic "Heroic epic"), such as the [Epic of Gilgamesh](/wiki/Epic_of_Gilgames "Epic of Gilgames"), [Fall of Jericho](/wiki/Fall_of_Jericho "Fall of Jericho") and [Conquest of Canaan](/wiki/Book_of_Joshua "Book of Joshua"), [Trojan War](/wiki/Trojan_War "Trojan War") in [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer")'s *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")*, and [Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata") (though their [historicity](/wiki/Historicity_of_the_Iliad "Historicity of the Iliad") has been challenged). More credible records of the Israelite military history from the conquest of [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") to the defeats by the [Assyrian](/wiki/New_Assyrian_Empire "New Assyrian Empire") and [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylonian_Empire "Babylonian Empire") Empires are in the [Biblical historical books](/wiki/Historical_books "Historical books") following the [Book of Joshua](/wiki/Book_of_Joshua "Book of Joshua").

Next were *[The Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)")* by [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") (484–425 BC) who is often called the "father of history",[[23]](#cite_note-23) and the *[History of the Peloponnesian War](/wiki/History_of_the_Peloponnesian_War "History of the Peloponnesian War")* by [Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides"). Despite being an [Athenian](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), Thucydides' impartiality allowed him to take advantage of his exile to research the war from different perspectives by carefully examining documents and interviewing eyewitnesses.[[24]](#cite_note-24) An approach centered on the analysis of a leader was taken by [Xenophon](/wiki/Xenophon "Xenophon") (430–355 BC) in *[Anabasis](/wiki/Anabasis_(Xenophon) "Anabasis (Xenophon)")*, recording the expedition of [Cyrus the Younger](/wiki/Cyrus_the_Younger "Cyrus the Younger") into [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"). And *[Anabasis of Alexander](/wiki/Anabasis_of_Alexander "Anabasis of Alexander")* described the expedition in the reverse direction. Greek historians of the 2nd century BC, such as [Polybius](/wiki/Polybius "Polybius"), and later Roman historians, such as [Sallust](/wiki/Sallust "Sallust"), [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy"), [Appian](/wiki/Appian "Appian") and [Cassius Dio](/wiki/Cassius_Dio "Cassius Dio"), wrote about wars of the [rise of Rome](/wiki/Rise_of_Rome "Rise of Rome") to the primacy over the [Mediterranean](/wiki/History_of_the_Mediterranean_region "History of the Mediterranean region"). The memoirs of the Roman [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar#Memoirs "Julius Caesar") (100–44 BC) enable a comparative approach for campaigns such as *[Commentarii de Bello Gallico](/wiki/Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico "Commentarii de Bello Gallico")* and *[Commentarii de Bello Civili](/wiki/Commentarii_de_Bello_Civili "Commentarii de Bello Civili")*.

East of the Mediterranean world, *[Arthashastra](/wiki/Arthashastra "Arthashastra")* in India and *[The Art of War](/wiki/The_Art_of_War "The Art of War")*, *[The Book of Lord Shang](/wiki/The_Book_of_Lord_Shang "The Book of Lord Shang")*, and less known but not less rich in military records *[Guanzi](/wiki/Guanzi_(text) "Guanzi (text)")* in China present strategic doctrines during the [Axial Age](/wiki/Axial_Age "Axial Age"). *[Records of the Grand Historian](/wiki/Records_of_the_Grand_Historian "Records of the Grand Historian")* by [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian") and *[Han Fei Zi](/wiki/Han_Fei_Zi "Han Fei Zi")* describe the [Warring States](/wiki/Warring_States "Warring States") of China and the former also its culmination in the [Qin wars of unification](/wiki/Qin_wars_of_unification "Qin wars of unification").

## Technological evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Technological evolution")]

Main article: [History of military technology](/wiki/History_of_military_technology "History of military technology")

> The nature of warfare *never* changes, only its superficial manifestations. [Joshua](/wiki/Joshua "Joshua") and [David](/wiki/David "David"), [Hector](/wiki/Hector "Hector") and [Achilles](/wiki/Achilles "Achilles") would recognize the combat that our soldiers and Marines have waged in the alleys of Somalia and Iraq. The uniforms evolve, bronze gives way to titanium, arrows may be replaced by laser-guided bombs, but the heart of the matter is still killing your enemies until any survivors surrender and do your will.

— [Ralph Peters](/wiki/Ralph_Peters "Ralph Peters")[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Rams%C3%A9s_II_en_Qadesh%2C_relieve_de_Abu_Simbel.jpg/250px-Rams%C3%A9s_II_en_Qadesh%2C_relieve_de_Abu_Simbel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rams%C3%A9s_II_en_Qadesh,_relieve_de_Abu_Simbel.jpg)

Relief of [Ramses II](/wiki/Ramses_II "Ramses II") located in [Abu Simbel](/wiki/Abu_Simbel "Abu Simbel") fighting at the [Battle of Kadesh](/wiki/Battle_of_Kadesh "Battle of Kadesh") on a [chariot](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot")

New weapons development can dramatically alter the face of war, the cost of warfare, the preparations, and the training of soldiers and leaders. A rule of thumb is that if your enemy has a potentially war winning weapon, you have to either match it or neutralize it.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Ancient era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ancient era")]

[Chariots](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot") originated around 2000 BC. The chariot was an effective, fast weapon; while one man controlled the maneuvering of the chariot, a second bowman could shoot arrows at enemy soldiers. These became crucial to the maintenance of several governments, including the [New Egyptian Kingdom](/wiki/New_Egyptian_Kingdom "New Egyptian Kingdom") and the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") and the nation states of the early to middle [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Some of the military unit types and technologies which were developed in the ancient world are:[[29]](#cite_note-29)

* [Slinger](/wiki/Sling_(weapon) "Sling (weapon)")
* [Hoplite](/wiki/Hoplite "Hoplite")
* [Auxiliaries](/wiki/Auxiliaries_(Roman_military) "Auxiliaries (Roman military)")
* [Infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry")
* [Archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery")
* [Chariots](/wiki/Chariots "Chariots")
* [Cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry")

For settled agrarian civilizations, the infantry became the core of military action. The infantry started as opposing armed groups of soldiers underneath commanders. The [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and early Romans used rigid, heavily armed [phalanxes](/wiki/Phalanx_formation "Phalanx formation"). The Macedonians and Hellenistic states would adopt phalanx formations with [sarissa](/wiki/Sarissa "Sarissa") pikemen. The [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Army "Roman Army") would later adopt more flexible [maniples](/wiki/Maniple_(military_unit) "Maniple (military unit)") from their neighbors which made them extremely successful in the field of battle. The kingdoms of the [Warring States](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") in East Asia also adopted infantry combat, a transition from chariot warfare from centuries earlier.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[Archers](/wiki/Archery "Archery") were a major component of many ancient armies, notably those of the Persians, Scythians, Egyptians, Nubians, Indians, Chinese, Koreans and Japanese.

[Cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") became an important tool. In the [Sicilian Expedition](/wiki/Sicilian_Expedition "Sicilian Expedition"), led by Athens in an attempt to subdue [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Italy "Syracuse, Italy"), the well-trained Syracusan cavalry became crucial to the success of the Syracusans. [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedon "Macedon") [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") effectively deployed his cavalry forces to secure victories. In battles such as the [Battle of Cannae](/wiki/Battle_of_Cannae "Battle of Cannae") of the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War"), and the [Battle of Carrhae](/wiki/Battle_of_Carrhae "Battle of Carrhae") of the [Roman-Persian Wars](/wiki/Roman-Persian_Wars "Roman-Persian Wars"), the importance of the cavalry would be repeated.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

There were also [horse archers](/wiki/Horse_archer "Horse archer"), who had the ability to shoot on horseback—the [Parthians](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire"), [Scythians](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians"), [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols"), and other various steppe people were especially fearsome with this tactic. By the 3rd–4th century AD, [heavily armored cavalry](/wiki/Heavy_cavalry "Heavy cavalry") became widely adopted by the Parthians, [Sasanians](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), [Byzantines](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire"), [Eastern Han dynasty](/wiki/Eastern_Han_dynasty "Eastern Han dynasty") and [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms"), etc.

The early [Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") developed the use of [chariots](/wiki/War_chariot "War chariot") in warfare. The [scythed chariot](/wiki/Scythed_chariot "Scythed chariot") was later invented in India and soon adopted by the [Persians](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[War elephants](/wiki/War_elephant "War elephant") were sometimes deployed for fighting in ancient warfare. They were first used in India and later adopted by the Persians. War elephants were also used in the [Battle of the Hydaspes River](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Hydaspes_River "Battle of the Hydaspes River"), and by [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal") in the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War") against the Romans.[[33]](#cite_note-33) One of the most important military transactions of the ancient world was [Chandragupta Maurya](/wiki/Chandragupta_Maurya "Chandragupta Maurya")'s gift of 500 elephants to [Seleucus I Nicator](/wiki/Seleucus_I_Nicator "Seleucus I Nicator").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Trireme.jpg/250px-Trireme.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trireme.jpg)

A Greek [trireme](/wiki/Trireme "Trireme")

[Naval warfare](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare") was often crucial to military success. Early navies used sailing ships without cannons; often the goal was to ram the enemy ships and cause them to sink. There was human oar power, often using slaves, built up to ramming speed. [Galleys](/wiki/Galley "Galley") were used in the [3rd millennium BC](/wiki/3000_BC "3000 BC") by the [Cretans](/wiki/Crete "Crete"). The Greeks later advanced these ships.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In 1210 BC, the first recorded naval battle was fought between [Suppiluliuma II](/wiki/Suppiluliuma_II "Suppiluliuma II"), king of the [Hittites](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites"), and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), which was defeated. In the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars"), the navy became of increasing importance.

[Triremes](/wiki/Trireme "Trireme") were involved in more complicated sea-land operations. [Themistocles](/wiki/Themistocles "Themistocles") helped to build up a stronger Greek navy, composed of 310 ships, and defeated the Persians at the [Battle of Salamis](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamis "Battle of Salamis"), ending the Persian invasion of Greece.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In the [First Punic War](/wiki/First_Punic_War "First Punic War"), the war between [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") and Rome started with an advantage to Carthage because of their naval experience. A Roman fleet was built in 261 BC, with the addition of the [corvus](/wiki/Corvus_(weapon) "Corvus (weapon)") that allowed Roman soldiers to board enemy ships. The bridge would prove effective at the [Battle of Mylae](/wiki/Battle_of_Mylae "Battle of Mylae"), resulting in a Roman victory.

The [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings"), in the 8th century AD, invented a ship propelled by oars with a dragon decorating the prow, hence called the [Drakkar](/wiki/Drekar "Drekar"). The 12th century AD [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") invented ships with watertight bulkhead compartments while the 2nd century BC [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") invented rudders and sculled oars for their warships.

[Fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") are important in warfare. Early [hill-forts](/wiki/Hill-fort "Hill-fort") were used to protect inhabitants in the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"). They were primitive forts surrounded by ditches filled with water. Forts were then built out of mud bricks, stones, wood, and other available materials. Romans used rectangular fortresses built out of wood and stone. As long as there have been fortifications, there have been contraptions to break in, dating back to the times of [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") and earlier. [Siege warfare](/wiki/Siege_warfare "Siege warfare") is often necessary to capture forts.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Middle-ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Middle-ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Takezaki_suenaga_ekotoba3.jpg/250px-Takezaki_suenaga_ekotoba3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takezaki_suenaga_ekotoba3.jpg)

Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") boarding [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") ships in 1281

Some of the military unit types and technologies which were used in the medieval period are:

* [Artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery")
* [Cataphract](/wiki/Cataphract "Cataphract")
* [Condottieri](/wiki/Condottieri "Condottieri")
* [Fyrd](/wiki/Fyrd "Fyrd")
* [Rashidun](/wiki/Rashidun_army "Rashidun army")
* [Mobile guard](/wiki/Mobile_guard "Mobile guard")
* [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk")
* [Janissary](/wiki/Janissary "Janissary")
* [Knight](/wiki/Knight "Knight") (see also: [Chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry"))
* [Crossbow](/wiki/Crossbow "Crossbow")
* [Pikeman](/wiki/Pikeman "Pikeman")
* [Samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai")
* [Sipahi](/wiki/Sipahi "Sipahi")
* [Trebuchet](/wiki/Trebuchet "Trebuchet")

[Bows](/wiki/Bow_(weapon) "Bow (weapon)") and [arrows](/wiki/Arrow_(weapon) "Arrow (weapon)") were often used by combatants. Egyptians shot arrows from chariots effectively. The [crossbow](/wiki/Crossbow "Crossbow") was developed around 500 BC in China, and was used heavily in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").[[39]](#cite_note-39) The English/Welsh [longbow](/wiki/Longbow "Longbow") from the 12th century also became important in the Middle Ages. It helped to give the English a large early advantage in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), even though the English were eventually defeated. The [Battle of Crécy](/wiki/Battle_of_Cr%C3%A9cy "Battle of Crécy") and the [Battle of Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt") are excellent examples of how to destroy an enemy using a longbow. It dominated battlefields for over a century.

### Gunpowder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Gunpowder")]

Main article: [History of gunpowder](/wiki/History_of_gunpowder "History of gunpowder")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ming_Dynasty_eruptor_proto-cannon.jpg/250px-Ming_Dynasty_eruptor_proto-cannon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ming_Dynasty_eruptor_proto-cannon.jpg)

Illustration of an "eruptor", a proto-cannon, capable of firing cast-iron bombs filled with gunpowder, from the 14th century Ming dynasty book Huolongjing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Cannon_pic.jpg/330px-Cannon_pic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cannon_pic.jpg)

A small [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War")-era cannon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/155fire.jpg/250px-155fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:155fire.jpg)

A 155 mm [M198 howitzer](/wiki/M198_howitzer "M198 howitzer") firing a shell

There is evidence for [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") evolving slowly from formulations by [Chinese alchemists](/wiki/Chinese_alchemy "Chinese alchemy") as early as the 4th century, at first as experiments for life force and metal transmutation, and later experiments as pyrotechnics and incendiaries. By the 10th century, the developments in gunpowder led to many new weapons that were improved over time.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Chinese used incendiary devices based on this in siege warfare against the Mongols starting in the mid 13th century. "Pots with wicks of flax or cotton were used, containing a combination of sulfur, saltpeter ([potassium nitrate](/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate")), [aconitine](/wiki/Aconitine "Aconitine"), oil, resin, ground charcoal and wax."[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Joseph Needham](/wiki/Joseph_Needham "Joseph Needham") argued the Chinese were able to destroy buildings and walls using such devices. Such experimentation was not present in Western Europe, where the combination of saltpeter, sulfur and charcoal were used exclusively for explosives and as a propellant in firearms. What the Chinese often referred to as the "fire drug" arrived in Europe, fully fleshed out, as gunpowder.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42)

[Cannons](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") were first used in Europe in the early 14th century, and played a vital role in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"). The first cannons were simply welded metal bars in the form of a cylinder, and the first cannonballs were made of stone. By 1346, at the Battle of Crécy, the cannon had been used; at the [Battle of Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt") they would be used again.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The first infantry firearms, from [fire lances](/wiki/Fire_lances "Fire lances") to [hand cannons](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon"), were held in one hand, while the [explosive charge](/wiki/Explosive_charge "Explosive charge") was ignited by a lit match or hot coal held in the other hand. In the mid-15th century came the [matchlock](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock"), allowing the gun to be aimed and fired while held steady with both hands, as used in the [arquebus](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus"). Starting about 1500, clever but complicated firing mechanisms were invented to generate sparks to ignite the powder instead of a lit match, starting with the [wheel lock](/wiki/Wheel_lock "Wheel lock"), [snaplock](/wiki/Snaplock "Snaplock"), [snaphance](/wiki/Snaphance "Snaphance"), and finally the [flintlock mechanism](/wiki/Flintlock_mechanism "Flintlock mechanism"), which was simple and reliable, becoming standard with the [musket](/wiki/Musket "Musket") by the early 17th century.

At the beginning of the 16th century, the first European [fire ships](/wiki/Fire_ship "Fire ship") were used. Ships were filled with flammable materials, set on fire, and sent to enemy lines. This tactic was successfully used by [Francis Drake](/wiki/Francis_Drake "Francis Drake") to scatter the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") at the Battle of [Gravelines](/wiki/Gravelines "Gravelines"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) and would later be used by the Chinese, Russians, Greeks, and several other countries in naval battles.

[Naval mines](/wiki/Naval_mines "Naval mines") were invented in the 17th century, though they were not used in great numbers until the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). They were used heavily in the [First](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") and Second World Wars. Air-deployed naval mines were used to mine the [North Vietnamese](/wiki/North_Vietnamese "North Vietnamese") port of [Haiphong](/wiki/Haiphong "Haiphong") during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). The [Iraqi Navy](/wiki/Iraqi_Navy "Iraqi Navy") of [Saddam Hussein](/wiki/Saddam_Hussein "Saddam Hussein") used naval mines extensively during the [Tanker War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War#Attacks_on_shipping "Iran–Iraq War"), as part of the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War").

The first navigable [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") was built in 1624 by [Cornelius Drebbel](/wiki/Cornelius_Drebbel "Cornelius Drebbel"), it could cruise at a depth of 15 feet (5 m). However, the first military submarine was constructed in 1885 by [Isaac Peral](/wiki/Isaac_Peral "Isaac Peral").[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The [*Turtle*](/wiki/American_Turtle "American Turtle") was developed by [David Bushnell](/wiki/David_Bushnell_(inventor) "David Bushnell (inventor)") during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). [Robert Fulton](/wiki/Robert_Fulton "Robert Fulton") then improved the submarine design by creating the [*Nautilus*](/wiki/Nautilus_(1800_submarine) "Nautilus (1800 submarine)").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The [Howitzer](/wiki/Howitzer "Howitzer"), a type of [field artillery](/wiki/Field_artillery "Field artillery"), was developed in the 17th century to fire high trajectory explosive shells at targets that could not be reached by flat trajectory projectiles.

Organizational changes resulting in better training and intercommunication, made the concept [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") possible, allowing the use of infantry, cavalry, and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") in a coordinated way.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bayonets also became of wide usage to infantry soldiers. [Bayonet](/wiki/Bayonet "Bayonet") is named after [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne "Bayonne"), France where it was first manufactured in the 16th century. It is used often in infantry charges to fight in hand-to-hand combat. General [Jean Martinet](/wiki/Jean_Martinet "Jean Martinet") introduced the bayonet to the French army. They were used heavily in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and continued to be used in modern wars like the [Invasion of Iraq](/wiki/Invasion_of_Iraq "Invasion of Iraq").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[Balloons](/wiki/Balloon_(aircraft) "Balloon (aircraft)") were first used in warfare at the end of the 18th century. It was first introduced in Paris of 1783; the first balloon traveled over 5 miles (8 km). Previously military [scouts](/wiki/Reconnaissance "Reconnaissance") could only see from high points on the ground, or from the mast of a ship. Now they could be high in the sky, signalling to troops on the ground. This made it much more difficult for troop movements to go unobserved.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

At the end of the 18th century, iron-cased [artillery rockets](/wiki/Artillery_rocket "Artillery rocket") were successfully used militarily in India against the British by [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan") of the [Kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore") during the [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars"). Rockets were generally inaccurate at that time, though [William Hale](/wiki/William_Hale_(British_inventor) "William Hale (British inventor)"), in 1844, was able to develop a better rocket. The new rocket no longer needed the [rocket stick](/w/index.php?title=Rocket_stick&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rocket stick (page does not exist)"), and had a higher accuracy.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In the 1860s there were a series of advancements in [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"). The first [repeating rifle](/wiki/Repeating_rifle "Repeating rifle") was designed in 1860 by a company bought out by [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester_Repeating_Arms_Company "Winchester Repeating Arms Company"), which made new and improved versions. [Springfield rifles](/wiki/Springfield_rifle "Springfield rifle") arrived in the mid-19th century also. [Machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") arrived in the late 19th century. [Automatic rifles](/wiki/Automatic_rifle "Automatic rifle") and [light machine guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun") first arrived at the beginning of the 20th century.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In the later part of the 19th century, the self-propelled [torpedo](/wiki/Torpedo "Torpedo") was developed. The [HNoMS Rap](/wiki/HNoMS_Rap_(1873) "HNoMS Rap (1873)") was the world's first torpedo boat.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Early guns and artillery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Early guns and artillery")]

Further information: [History of the firearm](/wiki/History_of_the_firearm "History of the firearm")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Siege_orleans.jpg/250px-Siege_orleans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siege_orleans.jpg)

The first Western image of a battle with [cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon"): the [Siege of Orléans](/wiki/Siege_of_Orl%C3%A9ans_(1428%E2%80%931429) "Siege of Orléans (1428–1429)") in 1429

The fire lance, the predecessor of the gun, was invented in China between the tenth and eleventh centuries. The barrel was originally designed out of bamboo shoots, later with metal. [Joseph Needham](/wiki/Joseph_Needham_Professor_of_Chinese_History,_Science,_and_Civilization "Joseph Needham Professor of Chinese History, Science, and Civilization") notes "all the long preparations and tentative experiments were made in China, and everything came to Islam and the West fully fledged, whether it was the fire lance or the explosive bomb, the rocket or the metal-barrel handgun and bombard."[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42) By the 1320s, Europe had guns, but scholars state that the exact time and method of migration from China remains a mystery. Evidence of firearms is found in Iran and Central Asia in the late fourteenth century. It was not until roughly 1442 that guns were referenced in India. Reliable references to guns in Russia begin around 1382.[[53]](#cite_note-Partington1999-53)

An illustration of a "pot-shaped gun" found in the Holkham Hall Milemete manuscript dated to 1326 shows earliest advent of firearms in European history. The illustration shows an arrow, set in the pot-shaped gun pointed directly at a structure. Archaeological evidence of such "gun arrows" were discovered in [Eltz Castle](/wiki/Eltz_Castle "Eltz Castle"), "dated by relation to a historical event (a feud with the Archbishop of Trier in 1331–36 leading to a siege), seem to confirm again that this was at least one of the types of guns like the Milemete used in these very early examples."[[54]](#cite_note-:2-54)

According to Peter Fraser Purton, the best evidence of the earliest gun in Europe is the Loshult gun, dated to the fourteenth century. Discovered in 1861, the Loshult was made of bronze measured 11.8 inches in length. A replica of the Loshult was created, using similar gunpowder compounds with present-day materials, to determine the effectiveness of the weapon. The Gunpowder Research Group, who designed the recreation, found that at high elevations, the Loshult could fire as far as 1300 meters.[[54]](#cite_note-:2-54) Though inaccurate, missing targets further than 200 meters, the Loshult could fire a range of projectiles such as arrows and shot.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42) It was determined that the Loshult could be effectively fired at ranks of soldiers and structures.

Written works from the Cabinet des Titres of the Imperial Library of Paris has found evidence of canons in France in 1338. The works illustrate canons being used on-board ships at the Rouen during that time. "...an iron Fire-arm, which was provided with forty-eight bolts, made of iron and freather; also one pound of saltpetre and half a pound of sulphur to make the powder propel arrows."[[55]](#cite_note-:0-55)

Researchers have been unable to determine the sizes of these cannons and others, outside the artifacts recovered. Sir [Henry Brackenbury](/wiki/Henry_Brackenbury "Henry Brackenbury") was able to surmise the approximate size of these cannons by comparing receipts for both the firearms and the corresponding amounts of gunpowder purchased. The receipts show a transaction for "25 Livres for 5 canons." Brackenbury was able to deduce, when comparing the costs of the cannons and the gunpowder apportioned, that they each iron cannon weighed approximately 25 lbs, while the brass cannons weighed roughly 22 lbs.[[55]](#cite_note-:0-55)

[Philip the Bold](/wiki/Philip_the_Bold "Philip the Bold") (1363–1404) is credited[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] with creating the most effective artillery power in Europe in the late fourteenth century, effectively creating the Burgundian estate. Philip's development of a large artillery army made the small country a reputable force against larger empires such as England and France.[*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*][[56]](#cite_note-56)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Philip had achieved this by establishing a large scale artillery manufacturing economy in Burgundy.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42) Philip used his new cache of artillery to help the French capture an English-held fortress of Odruik. The artillery used to take Odruik used cannonballs measuring to about 450 pounds.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42)

Large artillery was a major contributing factor to the fall of Constantinople at the hands of [Mehmed the Conqueror](/wiki/Mehmed_the_Conqueror "Mehmed the Conqueror") (1432–1481). Having resigned his position as ruler due to youth and inexperience in 1446, Mehmed moved to the Ottoman capital of Manisa.[[57]](#cite_note-57) After his father, [Murad II](/wiki/Murad_II "Murad II") died in 1451, Mehmed once again became Sultan. He turned his attention to claiming the Byzantine capital, Constantinople. Mehmed, like Philip, started mass-producing cannons by enticing craftsmen to his cause with money and freedom. For 55 days, Constantinople was bombarded with artillery fire, throwing cannonballs as large as 800 lbs at its walls. On 29 May 1453, Constantinople fell into Ottoman control.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42)

### Early firearm tactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Early firearm tactics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Manif._di_bruxelles_su_dis.di_bernart_von_orley%2C_IGMN144483%2C_1526-31.JPG/330px-Manif._di_bruxelles_su_dis.di_bernart_von_orley%2C_IGMN144483%2C_1526-31.JPG)](/wiki/File:Manif._di_bruxelles_su_dis.di_bernart_von_orley,_IGMN144483,_1526-31.JPG)

The [Battle of Pavia](/wiki/Battle_of_Pavia "Battle of Pavia") in 1525. [Heavy cavalry](/wiki/Heavy_cavalry "Heavy cavalry") and [Landsknecht](/wiki/Landsknecht "Landsknecht") mercenaries armed with [arquebuses](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus").

As guns and artillery became more advanced and prevalent, so too did the tactics by which they were implemented. According to Historian Michael Roberts "...a military revolution began with the broad adoption of firearms and artillery by late sixteenth-century European armies."[[58]](#cite_note-Stavros_2013-58) Infantry with firearms replaced cavalry. Empires adapted their strongholds to withstand artillery fire. Eventually drilling strategies and battlefield tactics were adapted for the evolution in firearms use.

In Japan, at the same time during the sixteenth-century, this military evolution was also taking hold. These changes included a universal adoption of firearms, tactical developments for effective use, logistical restructuring within the military itself, and "the emergence of centralized and political and institutional relationships indicative of the early modern order."[[58]](#cite_note-Stavros_2013-58)

Tactically, beginning with [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"), the technique known as "volleying" or countermarch drills were implemented.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42) Volley fire is an organized implementation of firearms, where infantry are structured in ranks. The ranks will alternate between loading and firing positions, allowing more consistent rates of fire and preventing enemies from taking over a position while members reload.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Nagashino_Teppo-Ashigaru.jpg/330px-Nagashino_Teppo-Ashigaru.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagashino_Teppo-Ashigaru.jpg)

The [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino") in 1575. Key to Oda success during the battle was the deployment of 10,000 [Ashigaru](/wiki/Ashigaru "Ashigaru") arquebusiers.

Historical evidence shows that Oda Nobunaga implemented his volley technique successfully in 1575, twenty years before evidence of such a technique is shown in Europe. The first indications of the countermarch technique in Europe was by Lord William Louis of Nassau (1538–1574) in the mid-1590s.[[58]](#cite_note-Stavros_2013-58)[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42)

Korea also seemed to be adapting the volley technique, earlier than even the Japanese. "Koreans seem to have employed some kind of volley principle with guns by 1447, when the Korean King Sejong the Great instructed his gunners to shoot their 'fire barrels' in squads of five, taking turns firing and loading."[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42)

This was on display during what [Kenneth Swope](/wiki/Kenneth_Swope "Kenneth Swope") called the First Great East Asian War, when Japan was trying to take control and subjugate Korea.[[59]](#cite_note-:02-59) [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") (1537–1598) made a failed invasion of Korea, which lasted six years, eventually pushed back by the Koreans with the aid of Ming China.[[42]](#cite_note-:14-42) Japan, using overwhelming firepower, had many early victories on the Korean peninsulas. Though the Koreans had similar manpower, "the curtain of arrows thrown up by defenders was wiped out by [Japanese] gunfire."[[58]](#cite_note-Stavros_2013-58) After the Japanese were finally pushed back in 1598, sweeping military reforms took place in Korea, largely based on updating and implementing the volley technique with firearms.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/The_Battle_of_Chaldiran%2C_Qajar_Iran%2C_19th_century.jpg/250px-The_Battle_of_Chaldiran%2C_Qajar_Iran%2C_19th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Battle_of_Chaldiran,_Qajar_Iran,_19th_century.jpg)

The advanced [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") weaponry (cannons and muskets wielded by [janissaries](/wiki/Janissaries "Janissaries")) was the deciding factor of the [Battle of Chaldiran](/wiki/Battle_of_Chaldiran "Battle of Chaldiran").

It was [Qi Jiguang](/wiki/Qi_Jiguang "Qi Jiguang"), a Ming Chinese General that provided the original treatise, disseminated to Koreans, that aided in this venture. In these manuals, Qi "...gave detailed instructions in the use of small group tactics, psychological warfare, and other 'modern' techniques."[[59]](#cite_note-:02-59) Qi emphasized repetitive drilling, dividing men into smaller groups, separating the strong from weak. Qi's ethos was one of synthesizing smaller groups, trained in various tactical formations, into larger companies, battalions and armies. By doing this they could "operate as eyes, hands, and feet..." aiding to overall unit cohesion.[[59]](#cite_note-:02-59)

### Modern technologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Modern technologies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Yokosuka_D4Y3_Suisei-Mitsubishi-aircraft-works-Japanese-plane-Zero-ca1945-GAG01.jpg/250px-Yokosuka_D4Y3_Suisei-Mitsubishi-aircraft-works-Japanese-plane-Zero-ca1945-GAG01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yokosuka_D4Y3_Suisei-Mitsubishi-aircraft-works-Japanese-plane-Zero-ca1945-GAG01.jpg)

[Yokosuka D4Y3](/wiki/Yokosuka_D4Y "Yokosuka D4Y"), a Japanese [Aircraft](/wiki/Military_aircraft "Military aircraft") used during WWII

At the start of the World Wars, various nations had developed weapons that were a surprise to their adversaries, leading to a need to learn from this, and alter how to combat them. [Flame throwers](/wiki/Flame_thrower "Flame thrower") were first used in the First World War. The French were the first to introduce the [armored car](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") in 1902. Then in 1918, the British produced the first [armored troop carrier](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier"). Many early [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") were proof of concept but impractical until further development. In World War I, the British and French held a crucial advantage due to their superiority in tanks; the Germans had only a few dozen A7V tanks, as well as 170 captured tanks. The British and French both had several hundred each. The French tanks included the 13 ton [Schneider CA1](/wiki/Schneider_CA1 "Schneider CA1"), with a 75 mm gun, and the British had the [Mark IV](/wiki/Mark_I_tank#Mark_IV "Mark I tank") and [Mark V](/wiki/Mark_I_tank#Mark_V_series "Mark I tank") tanks.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

On 17 December 1903, the [Wright Brothers](/wiki/Wright_Brothers "Wright Brothers") performed the first controlled, powered, heavier-than-air flight; it went 39 meters (120 ft). In 1907, the first [helicopter](/wiki/Helicopter "Helicopter") flew, but it was not practical for usage. [Aviation](/wiki/World_War_I_Aviation "World War I Aviation") became important in World War I, in which several [aces](/wiki/Flying_ace "Flying ace") gained fame. In 1911 an aircraft took off from a warship for the first time. Landings on a cruiser were another matter. This led to the development of an [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") with a decent unobstructed flight deck.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

[Chemical warfare](/wiki/Chemical_warfare "Chemical warfare") exploded into the public consciousness in World War I but may have been used in earlier wars without as much human attention. The Germans used gas-filled shells at the [Battle of Bolimov](/wiki/Battle_of_Bolimov "Battle of Bolimov") in January 1915. These were not lethal, however. In April 1915, the Germans developed a chlorine gas that was highly lethal, and used it to moderate effect at the [Second Battle of Ypres](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Ypres "Second Battle of Ypres"). Gas masks were invented in matter of weeks, and poison gas proved ineffective at winning battles. It was made illegal by all nations in the 1920s.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

World War II gave rise to even more technology. The worth of aircraft grew from mostly reconnaissance to strategic bombing and more. The worth of the aircraft carrier was proved in the battles between the United States and Japan like the [Battle of Midway](/wiki/Battle_of_Midway "Battle of Midway"). [Radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") was independently invented by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") and [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers"). It used [radio waves](/wiki/Radio_wave "Radio wave") to detect objects. [Molotov cocktails](/wiki/Molotov_cocktail "Molotov cocktail") were invented by General Franco in the Spanish Civil War, directing the Nationalists to use them against Soviet tanks in the assault on Toledo. The [atomic bomb](/wiki/Atomic_bomb "Atomic bomb") was developed by the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") and [dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") in 1945, quickly and controversially ending World War II.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the main powers engaged in a [Nuclear arms race](/wiki/Nuclear_arms_race "Nuclear arms race")[[64]](#cite_note-64) which comprised the making of atomic bombs, hydrogen bombs, and more advanced nuclear bombs. In the [space race](/wiki/Space_race "Space race"), both nations attempted to launch human beings into space, to the moon and send satellites. Other technological advances were centered on intelligence (like the [spy satellite](/wiki/Spy_satellite "Spy satellite")) and missiles ([ballistic missiles](/wiki/Ballistic_missile "Ballistic missile"), [cruise missiles](/wiki/Cruise_missile "Cruise missile")). The [nuclear submarine](/wiki/Nuclear_submarine "Nuclear submarine") was invented in 1955. This meant submarines no longer needed to surface as often, and could run more quietly. They evolved into underwater missile platforms[[65]](#cite_note-65) and completed what became called [nuclear triad](/wiki/Nuclear_triad "Nuclear triad").
