### Prehistoric warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Prehistoric warfare")]

Further information: [Prehistoric warfare](/wiki/Prehistoric_warfare "Prehistoric warfare")

Prehistoric warfare refers to war that occurred between societies without [recorded history](/wiki/Recorded_history "Recorded history"). The [Tollense valley battlefield](/wiki/Tollense_valley_battlefield "Tollense valley battlefield") is the oldest evidence of a large scale battle in Europe. More than 4,000 warriors fought in a battle on the site in the 13th century BC.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Ancient warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ancient warfare")]

Further information: [Ancient warfare](/wiki/Ancient_warfare "Ancient warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Ur_excavations_%281900%29_%2814767240352%29.jpg/250px-Ur_excavations_%281900%29_%2814767240352%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ur_excavations_(1900)_(14767240352).jpg)

The [Standard of Ur](/wiki/Standard_of_Ur "Standard of Ur") with depictions of war and peace, from the [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") city-state of [Ur](/wiki/Ur "Ur"), c. 2600 BC

Much of what we know of [ancient history](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") is the history of militaries: their conquests, their movements, and their technological innovations. There are many reasons for this. Kingdoms and empires, the central units of control in the ancient world, could only be maintained through military force. Due to limited agricultural ability, there were relatively few areas that could support large communities, therefore fighting was common.

The [Umma–Lagash war](/wiki/Umma%E2%80%93Lagash_war "Umma–Lagash war") was one of the first wars in recorded history, fought between the [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") city-states of [Lagash](/wiki/Lagash "Lagash") and [Umma](/wiki/Umma "Umma"). The border conflict over the fertile [Guedena](/wiki/Gu-Edin "Gu-Edin") region lasted for several generations.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

[Weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon") and [armor](/wiki/Armor "Armor"), designed to be sturdy, tended to last longer than other artifacts, and thus a great deal of surviving artifacts recovered tend to fall in this category as they are more likely to survive. Weapons and armor were also mass-produced to a scale that makes them quite plentiful throughout history, and thus more likely to be found in archaeological digs.

Such items were also considered signs of prosperity or virtue, and thus were likely to be placed in tombs and monuments to prominent warriors. And writing, when it existed, was often used for kings to boast of military conquests or victories.

Writing, when used by the common man, also tended to record such events, as major battles and conquests constituted major events that many would have considered worthy of recording either in an epic such as the [Homeric](/wiki/Homeric "Homeric") writings pertaining to the Trojan War, or even personal writings. Indeed, the earliest stories center on warfare, as war was both a common and dramatic aspect of life; the witnessing of a major battle involving many thousands of soldiers would be quite a spectacle, even today, and thus considered worthy both of being recorded in song and art, but also in realistic histories, as well as being a central element in a fictional work.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Assyrian_Relief_Attack_on_Enemy_Town_from_Kalhu_%28Nimrud%29_Central_Palace_reign_of_Tiglath-pileser_III_British_Museum_-_2.jpg/250px-Assyrian_Relief_Attack_on_Enemy_Town_from_Kalhu_%28Nimrud%29_Central_Palace_reign_of_Tiglath-pileser_III_British_Museum_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Assyrian_Relief_Attack_on_Enemy_Town_from_Kalhu_(Nimrud)_Central_Palace_reign_of_Tiglath-pileser_III_British_Museum_-_2.jpg)

Siege engine in [Assyrian](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire") relief of attack on an enemy town during the reign of [Tiglath-Pileser III](/wiki/Tiglath-Pileser_III "Tiglath-Pileser III"), 743–720 BC, from his palace at [Nimrud](/wiki/Nimrud "Nimrud")

Lastly, as [nation states](/wiki/Nation_states "Nation states") evolved and empires grew, the increased need for order and efficiency lead to an increase in the number of records and writings. Officials and armies would have good reason for keeping detailed records and accounts involving any and all things concerning a matter such as warfare that, in the words of [Sun Tzu](/wiki/Sun_Tzu "Sun Tzu"), was "a matter of vital importance to the state". For all these reasons, military history comprises a large part of ancient history.

Notable militaries in the ancient world included the [Egyptians](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), [Assyrians](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire"), [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), [Persians](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), Ancient [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") (notably the [Spartans](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") and [Macedonians](/wiki/Macedon "Macedon")), [Kushites](/wiki/Military_of_ancient_Nubia "Military of ancient Nubia"), [Indians](/wiki/Military_history_of_India "Military history of India") (notably the [Magadhas](/wiki/Magadha_(Mahajanapada) "Magadha (Mahajanapada)"), [Gangaridais](/wiki/Gangaridai "Gangaridai"), [Gandharas](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and [Cholas](/wiki/Cholas "Cholas")), Early Imperial [Chinese](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China") (notably the [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") and [Han](/wiki/Han_(Warring_States) "Han (Warring States)") dynasties), [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") Confederation, Ancient [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), and [Carthaginians](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage").

The [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent") of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") was the center of several prehistoric conquests. Mesopotamia was conquered by the [Sumerians](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), [Akkadians](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire"), [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia"), [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria") and Persians. [Iranians](/wiki/Iran "Iran") were the first nation to introduce [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") into their army.[[68]](#cite_note-Suren-Pahlav-68)

[Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") began growing as an ancient power, but eventually fell to the [Libyans](/wiki/Libya "Libya"), [Nubians](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia"), Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, Romans, [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab").

The earliest recorded battle in [India](/wiki/History_of_India "History of India") was the [Battle of the Ten Kings](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Ten_Kings "Battle of the Ten Kings"). The [Indian epics](/wiki/Indian_epic_poetry "Indian epic poetry") *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")* and *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")* are centered on conflicts and refer to military formations, theories of warfare and esoteric weaponry. [Chanakya](/wiki/Chanakya "Chanakya")'s *[Arthashastra](/wiki/Arthashastra "Arthashastra")* contains a detailed study on ancient warfare, including topics on [espionage](/wiki/Espionage "Espionage") and [war elephants](/wiki/War_elephant "War elephant").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Greek-Persian_duel.jpg/250px-Greek-Persian_duel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greek-Persian_duel.jpg)

Greek [hoplite](/wiki/Hoplite "Hoplite") (right) and Persian warrior depicted fighting, on an ancient [kylix](/wiki/Kylix "Kylix"), 5th century BC

[Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") invaded Northwestern India and defeated [King Porus](/wiki/King_Porus "King Porus") in the [Battle of the Hydaspes River](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Hydaspes_River "Battle of the Hydaspes River"). The same region was soon re conquered by [Chandragupta Maurya](/wiki/Chandragupta_Maurya "Chandragupta Maurya") after defeating the Macedonians and [Seleucids](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire"). He also went on to conquer the [Nanda Empire](/wiki/Nanda_Dynasty "Nanda Dynasty") and unify Northern India. Most of Southern Asia was unified under his grandson [Ashoka the Great](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka") after the [Kalinga War](/wiki/Kalinga_War "Kalinga War"), though the empire collapsed not long after his reign.

In China, the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") and [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") had risen and collapsed. This led to a [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period"), in which several states continued to fight with each other over territory. Philosopher-strategists such as [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius") and [Sun Tzu](/wiki/Sun_Tzu "Sun Tzu") wrote various manuscripts on ancient warfare (as well as international diplomacy).

The Warring States era philosopher [Mozi](/wiki/Mozi "Mozi") ([Micius](/wiki/Micius "Micius")) and his [Mohist](/wiki/Mohist "Mohist") followers invented various siege weapons and siegecraft, including the Cloud Ladder (a four-wheeled, extendable ramp) to scale fortified walls during a siege of an enemy city. The warring states were first unified by [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") after a series of military conquests, creating the first empire in China.

His [empire](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") was succeeded by the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), which expanded into Central [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), Northern China/Manchuria, Southern China, and present day Korea and Vietnam. The Han came into conflict with settled people such as the [Wiman Joseon](/wiki/Wiman_Joseon "Wiman Joseon"), and proto-Vietnamese Nanyue. They also came into conflict with the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") ([Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns")), [Yuezhi](/wiki/Yuezhi "Yuezhi"), and other steppe civilizations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Terracotta_Army_%286143564816%29.jpg/250px-Terracotta_Army_%286143564816%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terracotta_Army_(6143564816).jpg)

Lifelike soldier statues from the [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army"), discovered near modern [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), which was meant to guard the [Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_the_First_Qin_Emperor "Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor"), 3rd century BC

The Han defeated and drove the Xiongnus west, securing the city-states along the silk route that continued into the [Parthian](/wiki/Parthia "Parthia") Empire. After the decline of central imperial authority, the Han dynasty collapsed into an era of civil war and continuous warfare during the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period in the 3rd century AD.

The [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") Persian Empire was founded by [Cyrus the Great](/wiki/Cyrus_the_Great "Cyrus the Great") after conquering the [Median Empire](/wiki/Medes "Medes"), [Neo-Babylonian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_Empire "Neo-Babylonian Empire"), [Lydia](/wiki/Lydia "Lydia") and [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor"). His successor [Cambyses](/wiki/Cambyses_II_of_Persia "Cambyses II of Persia") went on to conquer the [Egyptian Empire](/wiki/Egyptian_Empire "Egyptian Empire"), much of Central Asia, and parts of Greece, India and Libya. The empire later fell to Alexander the Great after defeating [Darius III](/wiki/Darius_III_of_Persia "Darius III of Persia"). After being ruled by the Seleucid dynasty, the Persian Empire was subsequently ruled by the Parthian and [Sassanid](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") dynasties, which were the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire")'s greatest rivals during the [Roman-Persian Wars](/wiki/Roman-Persian_Wars "Roman-Persian Wars").

In [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), several [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state") rose to power, including [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") and [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta"). The Greeks successfully stopped two Persian invasions, the first at the [Battle of Marathon](/wiki/Battle_of_Marathon "Battle of Marathon"), where the Persians were led by [Darius the Great](/wiki/Darius_I_of_Persia "Darius I of Persia"), and the second at the [Battle of Salamis](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamis "Battle of Salamis"), a naval battle where the Greek ships were deployed by orders of [Themistocles](/wiki/Themistocles "Themistocles") and the Persians were under [Xerxes I](/wiki/Xerxes_I "Xerxes I"), and the land engagement of the [Battle of Plataea](/wiki/Battle_of_Plataea "Battle of Plataea").

The [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") then erupted between the two Greek powers Athens and Sparta. Athens built a long wall to protect its inhabitants, but the wall helped to facilitate the spread of a plague that killed about 30,000 Athenians, including [Pericles](/wiki/Pericles "Pericles"). After a disastrous campaign against [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Italy "Syracuse, Italy"), the Athenian navy was decisively defeated by [Lysander](/wiki/Lysander "Lysander") at the [Battle of Aegospotami](/wiki/Battle_of_Aegospotami "Battle of Aegospotami").

The [Macedonians](/wiki/Ancient_Macedonians "Ancient Macedonians"), underneath [Philip II of Macedon](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Macedon "Philip II of Macedon") and Alexander the Great, invaded Persia and won several major victories, establishing Macedonia as a major power. However, following Alexander's death at an early age, the empire quickly fell apart.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/10_2023_-_Palazzo_Altemps%2C_Roma%2C_Lazio%2C_00186%2C_Italia_-_Sarcofago_Grande_Ludovisi_%28Grande_Ludovisi_sarcophagus%29_-_Arte_Romana_-_Photo_Paolo_Villa_FO232047_ombre_gimp_bis.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_2023_-_Palazzo_Altemps,_Roma,_Lazio,_00186,_Italia_-_Sarcofago_Grande_Ludovisi_(Grande_Ludovisi_sarcophagus)_-_Arte_Romana_-_Photo_Paolo_Villa_FO232047_ombre_gimp_bis.jpg)

The 3rd-century [Great Ludovisi sarcophagus](/wiki/Great_Ludovisi_sarcophagus "Great Ludovisi sarcophagus") depicts a battle between [Romans](/wiki/Military_of_ancient_Rome "Military of ancient Rome") and [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths").

Meanwhile, Rome was gaining power, following a rebellion against the [Etruscans](/wiki/Etruscans "Etruscans"). During the three [Punic Wars](/wiki/Punic_Wars "Punic Wars"), the Romans defeated the neighboring power of Carthage. The [First Punic War](/wiki/First_Punic_War "First Punic War") centered on [naval warfare](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare"). The [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War") started with [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal")'s invasion of Italy by crossing the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"). He famously won the encirclement at the [Battle of Cannae](/wiki/Battle_of_Cannae "Battle of Cannae"). However, after [Scipio](/wiki/Scipio_Africanus "Scipio Africanus") invaded Carthage, Hannibal was forced to follow and was defeated at the [Battle of Zama](/wiki/Battle_of_Zama "Battle of Zama"), ending the role of Carthage as a power.

After defeating Carthage the Romans went on to become the Mediterranean's dominant power, successfully campaigning in Greece, ([Aemilius Paulus](/wiki/Aemilius_Paulus "Aemilius Paulus") decisive victory over Macedonia at the [Battle of Pydna](/wiki/Battle_of_Pydna "Battle of Pydna")), in the Middle East ([Lucius Licinius Lucullus](/wiki/Lucullus "Lucullus"), [Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus](/wiki/Gnaeus_Pompeius_Magnus "Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus")), in [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul") ([Gaius Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar")) and defeating several [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") ([Gaius Marius](/wiki/Gaius_Marius "Gaius Marius"), [Germanicus](/wiki/Germanicus "Germanicus")). While Roman armies suffered several major losses, their large population and ability (and will) to replace battlefield casualties, their training, organization, tactical and technical superiority enabled Rome to stay a predominant military force for several centuries, utilizing well trained and maneuverable armies to routinely overcome the much larger "tribal" armies of their foes (see Battles of [Aquae Sextiae](/wiki/Battle_of_Aquae_Sextiae "Battle of Aquae Sextiae"), [Vercellae](/wiki/Battle_of_Vercellae "Battle of Vercellae"), [Tigranocerta](/wiki/Battle_of_Tigranocerta "Battle of Tigranocerta"), [Alesia](/wiki/Battle_of_Alesia "Battle of Alesia")).

In 54 BC, the Roman triumvir [Marcus Licinius Crassus](/wiki/Marcus_Licinius_Crassus "Marcus Licinius Crassus") took the offensive against the [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire") in the east. In a decisive battle at [Carrhae](/wiki/Battle_of_Carrhae "Battle of Carrhae") Romans were defeated and the golden [Aquilae](/wiki/Aquila_(Roman) "Aquila (Roman)") (legionary battle standards) were taken as trophies to [Ctesiphon](/wiki/Ctesiphon "Ctesiphon"). The battle was one of the worst defeats suffered by the Roman Republic in its entire history.

While successfully dealing with foreign opponents, Rome experienced numerous civil wars, notably the power struggles of Roman generals such as Marius and [Sulla](/wiki/Sulla "Sulla") during the end of the Republic. Caesar was also notable for his role in the civil war against the other member of the Triumvirate (Pompey) and against the Roman Senate.

The successors of Caesar—Octavian and Mark Anthony—also fought a civil war with Caesar's assassins (Senators Brutus, Cassius, etc.). Octavian and Mark Anthony eventually fought another civil war between themselves to determine the sole ruler of Rome. Octavian emerged victorious and Rome was turned into an empire with a huge standing army of professional soldiers.

By the time of [Marcus Aurelius](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelius "Marcus Aurelius"), the Romans had expanded to the Atlantic Ocean in the west and to [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") in the east and controlled Northern Africa and Central Europe up to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). However, Aurelius marked the end of the [Five Good Emperors](/wiki/Nerva%E2%80%93Antonine_dynasty#Five_Good_Emperors "Nerva–Antonine dynasty"), and Rome quickly fell into decline.

The [Huns](/wiki/Hun "Hun"), [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), and other barbaric groups invaded Rome, which continued to suffer from [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") and other internal strifes. Despite the attempts of [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian"), [Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I_(emperor) "Constantine I (emperor)"), and [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I"), western Rome collapsed and was eventually conquered in 476. The [Byzantine empire](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") continued to prosper, however.

### Medieval warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Medieval warfare")]

Further information: [Medieval warfare](/wiki/Medieval_warfare "Medieval warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Battle_of_crecy_froissart.jpg/250px-Battle_of_crecy_froissart.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_crecy_froissart.jpg)

[Battle of Crécy](/wiki/Battle_of_Cr%C3%A9cy "Battle of Crécy") (1346) between the English and French in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War").

When [stirrups](/wiki/Stirrups "Stirrups") came into use some time during the [Dark Ages](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)") militaries were forever changed. This invention coupled with technological, cultural, and social developments had forced a dramatic transformation in the character of warfare from [antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), changing military tactics and the role of [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery").

Similar patterns of warfare existed in other parts of the world. In China around the 5th century armies moved from massed infantry to cavalry based forces, copying the [steppe](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe") [nomads](/wiki/Nomad "Nomad"). The Middle East and North Africa used similar, if often more advanced, technologies than Europe.

In Japan, the Medieval warfare period is considered by many to have stretched into the 19th century. In Africa along the [Sahel](/wiki/Sahel "Sahel") and [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan_(region) "Sudan (region)") states like the [Kingdom of Sennar](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sennar "Kingdom of Sennar") and [Fulani Empire](/wiki/Fulani_Empire "Fulani Empire") employed Medieval tactics and weapons well after they had been supplanted in Europe.

In the [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") period, [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") was firmly implanted, and there existed many landlords in Europe. Landlords often owned [castles](/wiki/Castle "Castle") to protect their territory.

The Islamic [Arab Empire](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate") began rapidly expanding throughout the Middle East, North Africa, and Central Asia, initially led by [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate"), and later under the [Umayyads](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate"). While their attempts to invade Europe by way of the Balkans were [defeated](/wiki/Siege_of_Constantinople_(717%E2%80%93718) "Siege of Constantinople (717–718)") by [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") and [Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire"),[[69]](#cite_note-69) the Arabs expanded to the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") in the west and the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") in the east. The [Abassids](/wiki/Abassid "Abassid") then took over the Arab Empire, though the Umayyads remained in control of [Islamic Spain](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus").

At the [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours"), the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") under [Charles Martel](/wiki/Charles_Martel "Charles Martel") stopped short a Muslim invasion. The Abassids defeated the [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") Chinese army at the [Battle of Talas](/wiki/Battle_of_Talas "Battle of Talas"), but were later defeated by the [Seljuk Turks](/wiki/Seljuk_Turk "Seljuk Turk") and the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") centuries later, until the Arab Empire eventually came to an end after the [Battle of Baghdad](/wiki/Battle_of_Baghdad_(1258) "Battle of Baghdad (1258)") in 1258.

In China, the [Sui dynasty](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") had risen and conquered the Chen dynasty of the south. They invaded Vietnam (northern Vietnam had been in Chinese control since the Han dynasty), fighting the troops of [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa"), who had cavalry mounted on elephants. After decades of economic turmoil and a [failed invasion of Korea](/wiki/Goguryeo-Sui_Wars "Goguryeo-Sui Wars"), the Sui collapsed and was followed by the Tang dynasty, who fought with various [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") groups, the [Tibetans](/wiki/Tibetan_people "Tibetan people") of [Lhasa](/wiki/Lhasa "Lhasa"), the [Tanguts](/wiki/Tanguts "Tanguts"), the [Khitans](/wiki/Khitan_people "Khitan people"), and collapsed due to political fragmentation of powerful regional military governors (jiedushi). The innovative [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") followed next, inventing new weapons of war that employed the use of [Greek Fire](/wiki/Greek_Fire "Greek Fire") and [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") (see section below) against enemies such as the [Jurchens](/wiki/Jurchens "Jurchens").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Autor_nieznany_%28malarz_z_kr%C4%99gu_Lukasa_Cranacha_Starszego%29%2C_Bitwa_pod_Orsz%C4%85.jpg/250px-Autor_nieznany_%28malarz_z_kr%C4%99gu_Lukasa_Cranacha_Starszego%29%2C_Bitwa_pod_Orsz%C4%85.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autor_nieznany_(malarz_z_kr%C4%99gu_Lukasa_Cranacha_Starszego),_Bitwa_pod_Orsz%C4%85.jpg)

The victory of the [Polish-Lithuanian](/wiki/Muscovite%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Wars "Muscovite–Lithuanian Wars") forces over the Muscovites at the [Battle of Orsha](/wiki/Battle_of_Orsha "Battle of Orsha") in 1514

The [Mongols](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") under [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan"), [Ögedei Khan](/wiki/%C3%96gedei_Khan "Ögedei Khan"), [Möngke Khan](/wiki/M%C3%B6ngke_Khan "Möngke Khan"), and [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") conquered most of Eurasia. They took over China, Persia, Turkestan, and Russia. After Kublai Khan took power and created the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), the divisions of the empire ceased to cooperate with each other, and the Mongol Empire was only nominally united.

In New Zealand, prior to European discovery, oral histories, legends and [whakapapa](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa") include many stories of battles and wars. [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") warriors were held in high esteem. One group of [Polynesians](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") migrated to the [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands"), where they developed the largely pacifist [Moriori](/wiki/Moriori_people "Moriori people") culture. Their pacifism left the Moriori unable to defend themselves when the islands were invaded by mainland Māori in the 1830s.

They proceeded to massacre the Moriori and enslave the survivors.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) [Warrior](/wiki/Warrior "Warrior") culture also developed in the isolated [Hawaiian Islands](/wiki/Hawaiian_Islands "Hawaiian Islands"). During the 1780s and 1790s the chiefs and alii were constantly fighting for power. After a series of battles the Hawaiian Islands were united for the first time under a single ruler who would become known as [Kamehameha I](/wiki/Kamehameha_I "Kamehameha I").

### Gunpowder warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Gunpowder warfare")]

Further information: [Gunpowder warfare](/wiki/Gunpowder_warfare "Gunpowder warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Sebastiaen_Vrancx_%281573-1647%29_-_Veldslag_%281640%29_-_Sevilla_Bellas_Artes_22-03-2011_12-07-38.jpg/250px-Sebastiaen_Vrancx_%281573-1647%29_-_Veldslag_%281640%29_-_Sevilla_Bellas_Artes_22-03-2011_12-07-38.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sebastiaen_Vrancx_(1573-1647)_-_Veldslag_(1640)_-_Sevilla_Bellas_Artes_22-03-2011_12-07-38.jpg)

[Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), or [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt") against Spain, painting by [Sebastiaen Vrancx](/wiki/Sebastiaen_Vrancx "Sebastiaen Vrancx")

After [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") weapons were first developed in [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") China (see also: [Technology of the Song dynasty](/wiki/Technology_of_the_Song_dynasty "Technology of the Song dynasty")), the technology later spread west to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), from where it spread to the [Safavid Empire](/wiki/Safavid_Empire "Safavid Empire") of Persia and the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") of India. The [arquebus](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus") was later adopted by European armies during the [Italian Wars](/wiki/Italian_Wars "Italian Wars") of the early 16th century.

This all brought an end to the dominance of armored cavalry on the battlefield. The simultaneous decline of the feudal system—and the absorption of the medieval city-states into larger states—allowed the creation of professional standing armies to replace the [feudal levies](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") and mercenaries that had been the standard military component of the Middle Ages.

In Africa, [Ahmad ibn Ibrihim al-Ghazi](/wiki/Ahmad_ibn_Ibrihim_al-Ghazi "Ahmad ibn Ibrihim al-Ghazi"), was the first African commander to use gunpowder on the continent in the [Ethiopian–Adal War](/wiki/Ethiopian%E2%80%93Adal_War "Ethiopian–Adal War"), that lasted for fourteen years (1529–1543).

The period spanning between the 1648 [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") and the 1789 [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") is also known as *[Kabinettskriege](/wiki/Kabinettskriege "Kabinettskriege")* (Princes' warfare) as wars were mainly carried out by imperial or monarchics states, decided by cabinets and limited in scope and in their aims. They also involved quickly shifting alliances, and mainly used mercenaries.

Over the course of the 18th–19th centuries all military arms and services underwent significant developments that included a more mobile [field artillery](/wiki/Field_artillery "Field artillery"), the transition from use of battalion [infantry drill](/w/index.php?title=Infantry_drill&action=edit&redlink=1 "Infantry drill (page does not exist)") in [close order](/wiki/Close_order_formation "Close order formation") to open order formations and the transfer of emphasis from the use of [bayonets](/wiki/Bayonets "Bayonets") to the rifle that replaced the musket, and virtual replacement of all types of cavalry with the universal [dragoons](/wiki/Dragoons "Dragoons"), or [mounted infantry](/wiki/Mounted_infantry "Mounted infantry").

### Military Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Military Revolution")]

Main article: [Military Revolution](/wiki/Military_Revolution "Military Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Assault_on_Coevorden_in_1672_-_De_bestorming_van_Coevorden%2C_30_december_1672_%28Pieter_Wouwerman%29.jpg/250px-Assault_on_Coevorden_in_1672_-_De_bestorming_van_Coevorden%2C_30_december_1672_%28Pieter_Wouwerman%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Assault_on_Coevorden_in_1672_-_De_bestorming_van_Coevorden,_30_december_1672_(Pieter_Wouwerman).jpg)

Dutch forces storming [Coevorden](/wiki/Coevorden "Coevorden") during the [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War"), 1672

The Military Revolution is a conceptual schema for explaining the transformation of European military strategy, tactics and technology in the early modern period.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The argument is that dramatic advances in technology, government finance, and public administration transformed and modernized European armies, tactics, and logistics. Since warfare was so central to the European state, the transformation had a major impact on modernizing government bureaucracies, taxation, and the national economy. The concept was introduced by [Michael Roberts](/wiki/Michael_Roberts_(historian) "Michael Roberts (historian)") in the 1950s as he focused on [Sweden 1560–1660](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire"). Roberts emphasized the introduction of muskets that could not be aimed at small targets, but could be very effective when fired in volleys by three ranks of infantry soldiers, with one firing while the other two ranks reloaded. All three ranks march forward to demolish the enemy. The infantry now had the firepower that had been reserved to the artillery, and had mobility that could rapidly advance in the battlefield, which the artillery lacked. The infantry thereby surpassed the artillery in tactical maneuvering on the battlefield. Roberts linked these advances with larger historical consequences, arguing that innovations in tactics, drill and doctrine by the Dutch and Swedes 1560–1660 led to a need for more and better trained troops and thus for permanent forces (standing armies). Armies grew much larger and more expensive. These changes in turn had major political consequences in the level of administrative support and the supply of money, men and provisions, producing new financial demands and the creation of new governmental institutions. "Thus, argued Roberts, the modern art of war made possible—and necessary—the creation of the modern state".[[73]](#cite_note-73) In the 1990s the concept was modified and extended by [Geoffrey Parker](/wiki/Geoffrey_Parker_(historian) "Geoffrey Parker (historian)"), who argued that developments in fortification and siege warfare caused the revolution. The concept of a military revolution based upon technology has given way to models based more on a slow evolution in which technology plays a minor role to organization, command and control, logistics and in general non-material improvements. The revolutionary nature of these changes was only visible after a long evolution that handed Europe a predominant place in warfare, a place that the industrial revolution would confirm.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The concept of a military revolution in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries has received a mixed reception among historians. Noted military historians Michael Duffy and [Jeremy Black](/wiki/Jeremy_Black_(historian) "Jeremy Black (historian)") have strongly criticised it as misleading, exaggerated and simplistic.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### Industrial warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Industrial warfare")]

Further information: [Industrial warfare](/wiki/Industrial_warfare "Industrial warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Bataille_Lizaine.jpg/250px-Bataille_Lizaine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bataille_Lizaine.jpg)

[Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War")

As weapons—particularly small arms—became easier to use, countries began to abandon a complete reliance on professional soldiers in favor of [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription"). Technological advances became increasingly important; while the armies of the previous period had usually had similar weapons, the industrial age saw encounters such as the [Battle of Sadowa](/wiki/Battle_of_Sadowa "Battle of Sadowa"), in which possession of a more advanced technology played a decisive role in the outcome.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Conscription was employed in industrial warfare to increase the number of military personnel that were available for combat. Conscription was notably used by [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") and the major parties during the two World Wars.

[Total war](/wiki/Total_war "Total war") was used in industrial warfare, the objective being to prevent the opposing nation to engage in war. Napoleon was the innovator.[[78]](#cite_note-78) [William Tecumseh Sherman](/wiki/William_Tecumseh_Sherman "William Tecumseh Sherman")'s "[March to the Sea](/wiki/Sherman%27s_March_to_the_Sea "Sherman's March to the Sea")" and [Philip Sheridan](/wiki/Philip_Sheridan "Philip Sheridan")'s burning of the [Shenandoah Valley](/wiki/Shenandoah_Valley "Shenandoah Valley") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") were examples.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) On the largest scale the [strategic bombing of enemy cities and industrial factories](/wiki/Strategic_bombing_during_World_War_II "Strategic bombing during World War II") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") was total warfare.

### Modern warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_history&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Modern warfare")]

Further information: [Modern warfare](/wiki/Modern_warfare "Modern warfare")

Since the 1940s, preparation for a major war has been based on technological arms races involving all sorts of new weapons systems, such as nuclear and biological, as well as computerized control systems, and the opening of new venues, such as seen in the [Space race](/wiki/Space_race "Space race") involving the United States, the Soviet Union, and more recently, China.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Modern war also saw the improvement of [armored tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank") technology. While tanks were present in the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), armored warfare technology came to a head with the start of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). Many of the technologies commonly seen on main battle tanks today, such as [composite armor](/wiki/Composite_armour "Composite armour"), [high caliber cannons](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun"), and [advanced targeting systems](/wiki/Tank#Active_protection_system "Tank"), would be developed during this time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A distinctive feature since 1945 is the decline in number and casualties of interstate wars. Instead actual fighting has largely been a matter of civil wars and insurgencies.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The major exceptions were the [Indo-Pakistani War of 1971](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971"), the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War") 1980–1988, the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") of 1990–91, and the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War").
